videos.transcript: What's up guys! We're here in Seville, and in this video, we're going to show you our top things to do here. So hope you enjoy it! Seville, also called Sevilla in Spanish, is located in the south of Spain. This vibrant capital of the Andalucía region is known for its rich history, stunning architecture, and lively culture. Add to that a warm and dry climate and you have a year-round travel destination. In this video, we highlight 10 of the best things to do in Seville. Now let's dive in! We are Zac and Ine. We make helpful videos about destinations around the world so you can easily plan your own adventures. First, we recommend going inside one of the most beautiful buildings in the city. This 16th century residence was restored by the art-loving Countess of Lebrija in 1901. The building itself is decorated with ornamental features, such as Moorish-style arches. You can find an impressive art collection here that was built over a lifetime. There are two floors: the ground floor houses Roman mosaics, sculptures, paintings and other objects. The upper floor has more preserved rooms which are accessible via stunning staircase and can only be visited by guided tour every hour. Although we weren't allowed to film or take pictures inside the upper rooms, it's definitely still worth visiting that area. Admire Seville from above by climbing up the Giralda Tower. Part of the Seville Cathedral complex, this bell tower was once a minaret. You'll wind your way up a gentle ramp that once allowed horses to reach the top. From here, you can enjoy panoramic vistas of Seville's terracotta rooftops, the winding Guadalquivir River, and beyond. Combine the tower with a visit to the cathedral, a UNESCO World Heritage Site that showcases stunning Gothic architecture in rich artworks. As this is a popular thing to do in Seville, it's important to buy your tickets in advance. Wander the charming maze of Santa Cruz, Seville's former Jewish Quarter. Located in the heart of the city, this historic district boasts narrow winding streets adorned with whitewashed houses and colorful flower pots. Find hidden squares, soak in the unique atmosphere, indulge in the tapas culture, browse local shops for souvenirs, or simply get lost in the streets. Unwind and discover Seville's highlights on a scenic river cruise. For 1 hour, you glide past iconic landmarks like the Triana neighborhood, Triana bridge, and the Torre del Oro. All while enjoying informative commentary and refreshing drinks from the onboard bar. We opted for an eco-friendly boat with comfortable seating and left a link to the one we booked in the description below the video. Across the river from Seville's city center lies Triana, a vibrant and historic neighborhood. Known for its flamenco shows, Triana pulses with energy. Walk around its colorful streets which are adorned with ceramic tiles and soak in the artistic atmosphere. Don't miss the Mercado, which is filled with a variety of food stalls. The highlight of this area is the lively riverside promenade, lined with charming restaurants and stunning views of Seville's skyline. If you're only visiting Seville on your trip, we highly recommend escaping the hustle and bustle at least once. Two of southern Spain's finest gems are located less than 2 hours away: Ronda and Setenil de las Bodegas. Setenil de las Bodegas is a small village of whitewashed houses built directly into the surrounding cliffs, creating a dramatic and picturesque scene. Ronda is a small city known for its cliffside setting in a deep gorge. The most iconic landmark here is the Puente Nuevo bridge, a feat of engineering that soars over the gorge. You can combine both places on a day trip from Seville and we left a link in the description below the video so you can check out tour options. The Metropol Parasol, also known as Las Setas or The Mushrooms, claims to be the largest wooden structure in the world. Consisting of six mushroom-shaped parasols, this unique landmark is organized in different levels. The underground level houses the Antiquarium, where Roman and Moorish remains are displayed. The ground level is home to a food market, and the upper levels are home to panoramic terraces and an immersive video experience. You can admire the structure from below for free, but the most impressive views are at the very top along the panoramic walkway, which you have to pay for. The Royal Alcazar is a stunning palace complex that's been in use for over 1,000 years. Originally built as a fortress by the Moors, it was later expanded and renovated by Christian monarchs. Today, the architecture is a unique blend of Moorish, Renaissance, and Baroque styles, showcasing the rich cultural history of the region. Take time to explore the elaborate courtyards, decorated halls, intricate tilework, and lush gardens throughout the palace grounds. This is a vast complex and takes at least 2 to 3 hours to explore. As the Alcazar is Seville's most popular tourist attraction, we again recommend booking your ticket well in advance. If you don't get a guide, it's best to get an audio guide, because there's a lot of history in this place. We paid about 7€ for one and we just shared it with the two of us. But you can also download the App and save a little bit of money. Stretching along the river, Maria Luisa Park is Seville's green oasis. This beautiful park boasts a unique blend of French and Spanish influences. Relax amidst fountains, ponds, and colorful flower beds, or simply stroll around the park's various pathways. This park offers an escape from the city's buzz perfect for a leisurely walk or a picnic under the Andalucían sun. Located inside Maria Luisa Park, you'll find the Plaza de España, a grand masterpiece of Spanish Renaissance architecture. This impressive semicircular plaza, which was built for the 1929 Ibero-American Exposition, is a true icon of the city. Each province of Spain is represented by pavilions adorned with colorful ceramics. The canals and small bridges complete the romantic scene. It's free to walk around and marvel at the architectural details. If you're going to more destinations in Andalucía, make sure to check out our video about the top places to visit in this enchanting region of Spain. We included can't miss places, some hidden gems, and important travel tips for this area. And of course there are a few other things to do in Seville that we didn't mention. We've put a list of those on the screen here for you. If you're planning your Spain trip, we recommend keeping things organized with our interactive travel planner. Find a discount code in the description below the video. We also included other links in the description that will help you with your Seville experience, such as an interactive map so you can get to each place that we mentioned. We'll catch you on the next adventure!

videos.transcript: With impressive architecture, flower filled courtyards, fragrant orange trees and an abundance of tapas bars, Sevilla, or Seville if you're an English speaker like me, is the perfect location for a sun-drenched city break. Based on our own trip to southern Spain we've created this three-day suggested itinerary covering some of the best things to see and do on your trip to Seville. On your first morning visit Plaza de Espana. If you're looking for breakfast first then we recommend L'Orangier. They had a variety of tostadas on offer so the friendly owner recommended a couple for us to try. Not only was this Spanish staple a delicious way to start the day but the welcoming owner was really wonderful. If you look on tripadvisor you'll see that it wasn't just us who had a positive experience as almost every single review refers to the warm service. After breakfast walk around 20 minutes to Plaza de Espana. Built around 100 years ago this picture-perfect crescent-shaped building is an incredibly pretty spot to while away a couple of hours. Feel the cooling mist of the giant fountain, listen to the clip-clop of the horses and watch people row boats along the waterway which is crisscrossed by four bridges representing the ancient kingdoms of Spain. The iconic plaza has hosted many events and even featured in films such as Lawrence of Arabia and Star Wars Episode 2. Not only is this plaza spectacular to observe from a distance but there's also so much beauty in the details. Look down to see the tiles on the floor, enjoy the decorative ceramics and rest your feet in one of 48 alcoves, each one showcasing a different province in Spain. You might catch a free flamenco performance while you're there so grab yourself a spot in the shade and enjoy the show. After exploring the grand verandas walk the upper floor to take in the views of the buzzing plaza and all the activity that's going on below. Opposite the square is Parque de María Luisa, the most famous park in Seville . With the tree-lined pathways, the water features, the colourful flowers and the wildlife this has to be one of the most beautiful parks I have been lucky enough to have visited. Take your time strolling around the shady park. It's the perfect place to rest, enjoy a picnic lunch and listen to the sound of the birds singing all around you. After spending the morning soaking in the plaza and the park enjoy the pleasant walk to Santa Cruz, the old Jewish quarter of Seville. This vibrant neighborhood is a warren of narrow streets, shops and tapas bars. While you wander this colourful maze look out for the Callejón Del Agua, which translates to water alley. Pipes in the wall of this street used to carry water to the Alcazar gardens, which is why it's known as the water alley. At the end of the street is Plaza Alfaro you might see people taking photos of this rather dreamy looking balcony, which is believed to have inspired the Barber of Seville. i'll admit i know pretty much nothing about opera but it's still worth stopping to admire Rosina's balcony. Around mid-afternoon visit the Royal Alcazar, a UNESCO world heritage site that is a must do on your visit to Seville. The Alcazar is incredibly popular so book your ticket online in advance to avoid the long queue. For over 1,000 years this site has been a royal residence and is still used by the Spanish royal family today, making it the oldest active royal palace in Europe. In its grand complex of palaces, courtyards and lush gardens there is a merging of Mudéjar, Islamic, gothic and renaissance styles influenced by the different rulers over the centuries. More recently the alcazar was understandably used as the stunning water gardens of Dorne in Game of Thrones. While you explore notice the exquisite detail all around you including on the floors and the ceilings. Take your time strolling the extensive gardens filled with geometric pruned hedges, colourful flowers lush greenery and ornate tiles. Linger around the peaceful ponds and listen to the gentle sound of trickling water. The gardens are patrolled by royal peacocks. You might hear them before you see them. While we tried to not go too close to the peacocks to avoid disturbing them this guy did not really give us a choice. You're being followed! Before visiting I'd read to give ourselves around one to two hours at the alcazar, however we were easily there for at least three.I would therefore suggest allocating around three hours to your visit, especially if you opt for the additional ticketed option to visit the upper royal room. By now you're probably ready for a rest whether that be relaxing in the gorgeous Murillo gardens behind the Royal Alcazar, popping back to your accommodation to freshen up or sitting at a bar with an ice-cold drink. If you're looking for dinner in the area then we really enjoyed Bodagita Romero, a traditional tapas bar known for serving the best pringa in Seville. This is a small sandwich filled with stewed pork chorizo and blood sausage. We also enjoyed stewed beef cheek and this spinach and chickpea dish which ended up being a firm favorite of ours that we ordered over and over at tapas bars throughout our trip. We'll be covering more local dishes to try and Seville plus other tips and information for planning your trip in an upcoming video so if you found this video helpful then do stick around for that. If you find you're covering our itinerary at a quicker pace or you simply want to switch something that we suggested out for a different activity then I will mention a couple of additional options at the end of each day that you could look into further. On day one you could visit Hospital de los Venerables in Santa Cruz. Formally a hospital, today it houses a collection of masterpieces from the Spanish golden age. A second option is back in Maria Louisa park where you could visit the Museum of Popular Arts and Traditions, dedicated to the ethnological heritage of Andalusia. Let's kick off day two by exploring the laid-back Triana district. The birthplace of famous flamenco artists, bullfighters and potters, this charismatic neighborhood feels worlds away from the tourist hub of Seville center. Start by crossing Triana bridge that connects the center of Seville with Triana. This is currently the oldest iron bridge in Spain. After crossing the bridge drop down into Triana market. We would advise starting your day here as even though on Google the market shows as being open until 5 pm when we arrived after 12 pm several stalls had already closed down. The local food market is packed with vendors selling fresh fruits and vegetables, meat and fish with some restaurants available if you want to try the food. we were really happy to find a store selling fresh mango juice - perfect on a hot day. Walk about two minutes from Triana market to the ceramics museum. Set in a restored ceramics factory here you can learn about the history of Triana's tile production. I particularly enjoyed looking at these unique ceramic advertising signs. This is a fairly small museum so you'll probably only spend about an hour here. Entry is included with your Royal Alcazar ticket and it's still valid the next day so make sure you bring it with you. Close by is St George's castle which is also included in your ticket, however it was closed during our visit. You can still walk down the Alley of Inquisition, formerly a part of St George's Castle where prisoners used to be taken to be judged by the courts of inquisition. Follow the river and cross back over the next bridge to see Torre del Orro. This military observation tower offers lovely views down the river and there's even a small naval museum inside. From Torre del Orro have a pleasant stroll along the river back up towards Triana bridge. From this side of the river you'll have great views of Calle Betis, a street made famous for its picturesque colourful facades and lively bars and restaurants. It's a lovely place to stop and relax with a drink and views over the river if you do get the chance. If you're looking for food while exploring Triana, while Calle Betis is a popular option. Our favorite meal of the trip was Bar Triana a small friendly tapas bar offering excellent food for great value. The chicken with whiskey sauce that our server recommended was so good. We enjoyed it that much that we actually returned here for our final night. Once you've had a leisurely lunch and rested your feet meander through the beautiful streets to the 15th century Palacio de las Duenas. This palace is a mixture of gothic and mudejar style and was a much loved home of one of Spain's most famous aristocrats: Duchess De Alba. As you enter you'll be greeted with lush gardens and the jaw-dropping palace facade. While it is interesting to walk around the palace rooms filled with various paintings and tapestries and memorabilia, for us it had to be the gardens that were the highlight. Stroll past citrus trees, flowers, water features and this absolute whopper of a tree. Just take a seat and enjoy the tranquility. Included in the cost of your ticket is an audio guide that you can access using your phone so you might find it helpful to take headphones with you. After spending around two hours at the palace walk just five minutes to the Antiquarium; an archaeological museum that sits below the Metropol Parasol, more commonly known as Las Setas, which we'll come back to shortly. Like the Ceramic Museum and St George's Castle the antiquarium is also included with your Royal Alcazar ticket. This small museum showcases the Roman and Moorish ruins that were unearthed when the Metropol Parasol was constructed. Raised walkways and glass flooring allow you to get a good view over the remains. Here you'll see houses with columns, wells and intricate mosaics. You will probably need around 45 minutes for your visit. At this point you could either head straight up Las Setas or stop for a food and drink break first. If you are looking for food and drink in the area then we would recommend La Sede. This small tapas bar is run by three brothers who serve up a mouth-watering tuna tartar on a bed of guacamole. Shaped like interconnecting mushrooms Las Setas claims to be the largest wooden structure in the world and offers some great views over the rooftops of Seville. If you visit once the sun has gone down then the wooden walkways are transformed with an ambient sound and light show. It does cost a little more to visit at night than during the day so if you're really not bothered about the light show then you could go earlier and save a bit of money. We managed to accidentally time our visit to catch both sunset and the light show which was pretty awesome, but i'll cover attraction ticket options in more detail in our upcoming planning and tips video. Once back at ground level if you're looking for somewhere for drinks and perhaps something else to eat then in under 10 minutes you could be at Alameda De Hercules. This popular tree-lined plaza is filled with bars and restaurants so grab yourself a beer and enjoy the lively atmosphere. A couple of extra popular attractions that you could consider for day two are the Seville Museum of Fine Arts or the bull ring where you could tour the arena and museum without needing to watch a bull fight. Now for day three before heading to your first attraction let's grab breakfast stop at Bar El Commercio, a popular choice for churros which you can dunk in their mugs of warm melted chocolate. Be warned these churros are absolutely huge but it will help fuel you up for the busy day ahead. Once you're stuffed full of churros roll to the enormous Seville Cathedral. Built on the site of the city's former mosque, Seville Cathedral is the largest gothic church and the third largest church in the world. It took over 100 years to build and houses over 80 different chapels. Not only is the sheer scale of this UNESCO World Heritage Site impressive, but the ornate interior is completely breathtaking. Gaze up at the magnificent gold altarpiece and look out for the tomb of Christopher Columbus. Climb the bell tower of the cathedral known as La Giralda. La Giralda used to be part of the great mosque of Seville before it was transformed into the Moorish bell tower that we see today. Instead of steps leading to the top La Giralda has 35 steadily inclining ramps. This was so the sultan could ride his horse to the top rather than taking the stairs, so if you're happy you don't have to climb a load of steps on your visit then that's who you can thank. While visiting the cathedral you could pay extra to book onto a rooftop tour. During this 90-minute tour a guide covers the history and architecture of the cathedral while giving you plenty of time to walk around and take in the panoramic views. Not only is walking around on the roofs of the cathedral a unique experience but you will also see the cathedral's interior from a different perspective as you cross the walkways from one staircase to another. There are only a limited number of small group tours per day so if you would like to take a rooftop tour then I would recommend booking in advance. You may have to be flexible about when you fit the tour into your itinerary as it will only be available at a few set times throughout the day. We thought it was worth the extra cost as it was one of the highlights of our trip to Seville. If you do decide to skip the rooftop tour then you could instead spend more time exploring the shop lined streets of the historic city centre. Around five minutes from the Seville cathedral is the Church of the Divine Saviour, which is included in your cathedral and your giralda ticket. Built on the site of a former mosque it is now a roman catholic church. The pink church overlooks the lively Plaza Del Salvador and inside there are beautiful stained glass windows and an elaborate altar. Next walk around 10 minutes to the breathtaking Casa de Pilatos. The 16th century civil palace was built on the order of Don Pedro Enriquez and later his son Fadrique Enriquez De Ribeira. Casa de Pilatos was named after Pontius Pilate, inspired by Fadrique's pilgrimage to Jerusalem. This gem of a building is a blend of Italian renaissance, mudejar and gothic styles. Once you arrive you'll enter an absolutely stunning courtyard flanked by marble statues and busts of spanish kings, roman emperors and other historic figures. From here explore the beautifully decorated rooms and serene gardens. Look out for the coats of arms on the ceiling of the praetor's room, the extravagant ceiling in the golden room, and of course this amazing staircase made of marble and surrounded by tiles. Just like Palacio de las Duenas, entrance includes an audio guide that you can listen to using your phone. Plan to spend around an hour here. if you enjoyed your taster Plaza de Espana then we would recommend ending your night to the flamenco show. You are in the birthplace of flamenco after all. We went to see a show at the Casa del Flamenco in the Santa Cruz neighborhood. Set in an intimate courtyard, no microphones or amps were used so the sound was raw and natural. We were blown away by the talent of the artist and I found myself completely drawn in by the performance. Alternative options for day three could include the flamenco museum or the archive of the Indies, where the records of Spain's colonial history are held. If you're spending longer in Seville and are up for venturing just outside of the city then the roman ruins of Italica are well worth the half-day visit. Walking through the excavated remains of the old Roman city is fascinating but the highlight has to be the 25,000 seat amphitheatre. In imperial times shows held here would last for several days. In the mornings there would be animal fights, where prisoners sentenced to death would be pitted against wild hungry animals. In the afternoons, following an intermission of executions and burlesque shows, would be the brutal gladiator fights. This amphitheater was also another Game of Thrones filming location; it was transformed into the dragon pit in King's Landing where the dragons of House Targaryen were held. If you do decide to visit Italica it's an easy bus ride from the Plaza De Armes bus station in Seville and there's no need to book a ticket in advance. If you enjoyed this video and want more information for your trip to Seville then I'll link our planning video here once it's ready. We also have a video coming up from the beautiful city of Cordoba, which you might be interested in if you're looking for day trips from Seville or for more destinations to add to your Andalusia itinerary. Thanks for watching, I hope this video was helpful.

videos.transcript: if you're planning a trip to Seville then this travel guide will cover the practical information and tips you need to have an amazing city break. Let's start with when to go. While you will have a great time in Seville no matter when you visit there are some things to consider when booking your trip. For many the best times to visit are from March to May. Typically the temperatures will be very comfortable although we all know with weather that there are never any guarantees. These excellent weather conditions do of course mean that this is peak season so things will be busier. While you may want to avoid the stifling summer like spring autumn can be a great time to visit as you'll dodge the intense heat of July and August, although rainfall levels do increase in October. The winter months could be a good option as temperatures are relatively mild and crowds will be lower, however daylight hours will be shorter. If you do want to visit for the lovely spring temperatures and long daylight hours be aware that two extremely popular events are held at this time of year: Semana Santa and the April Fair. Semana Santa takes place the week running up to Easter weekend with the April fair taking place two weeks after that. While either could be incredible to experience I would suggest researching them further and if you aren't interested in these events then avoid those dates as things will be busier and accommodation more expensive. After visiting in May ourselves I would recommend a late spring visit for one extra reason that we were not anticipating. As if Seville wasn't already picturesque enough after arriving we realized the city was filled with these absolutely beautiful trees covered in purple flowers. They seem to be dotted around everywhere - in the parks, the gardens, the plazas - even lining the busy roads. After Googling them we found out that these trees are called jacarandas and they burst with colour in May and then continue to bloom for a few weeks. Number two is how long to stay. I would recommend visiting Seville for at least three full days. This will give you enough time to see the main sights plus a couple of lesser visited attractions without having to rush. if you're visiting in the height of Summer then because of the heat you may want to take things at a slower pace than you normally would for a city break so do bear that in mind when planning your trip. We do have another video walking you through a suggested three-day itinerary for Seville which I will link at the end of this video. You have a number of good options for where to stay in Seville. First there is Santa Cruz and the center. These areas are best for first visits or shorter stays. Here you will be conveniently located for visiting the main sights and there's lots of cafes, tapas bars, shops and a wide range of accommodation. The Santa Cruz area is absolutely beautiful however as it is popular with tourists it could be a little loud which may bother you if you have a room facing the street. Once the city's port, El Arenal is also a good option for first-time visitors or short stays. This area is very pretty, it's perfect for riverside walks and there's plenty of restaurants, and is conveniently located close to many of Seville's attractions. You could stay in the Triana neighborhood which is where we stayed. It has more of a chilled local vibe and is certainly less touristy. Here there are some good budget options for accommodation and food, however depending on where in the neighborhood you stay it could still be loud until late so make sure you check the accommodation reviews. It is also further away from many of the main sights. We did find ourselves needing to pop back to the room on a couple of occasions and then wishing we were staying a little closer to the center although overall we did really enjoy staying in Triana. Around the Alameda area is relaxed and great for younger travelers or people who want to experience the nightlife. It's budget friendly with laid-back cafes and art spaces and bars, however like Triana this area is further from the main attractions than the likes of Santa Cruz, although you can still walk. What sights should you prioritize? While I would recommend a three or four day visit, if you do only have one day I would prioritize the following activities: visit the Real Alcazar which is the oldest active palace in Europe. If you only paid to go inside one attraction in Seville the Real Alcazar should be it. Explore the colourful Santa Cruz neighborhood which is the Jewish quarter of Seville. Visit the gorgeous Park de Maria Louisa where you can stroll under the beautiful shady trees and spend some time at the famous Plaza de Espana and last but definitely not least go inside the grand Cathedral and climb up the Moorish Bell Tower. If you're traveling by plane then the cheapest way to get to and from Seville International Airport is by bus. The bus stop at the airport is located straight outside the main terminal and takes around 30 minutes to reach the city center. The bus stops at several locations in the city between Santa Justa train station and Plaza de Armas bus station which is where the bus terminates. It costs 4 Euros one way and you can buy your ticket on the bus. Alternatively you can take a taxi to the Center for 20 to 30 Euros depending on exactly where you're going and the time of day. For getting around the best way to explore Seville is on foot. In the Central Area the majority of the sites are all reasonably close together plus walking the beautiful streets is part of the fun. However if you do visit in the summer months then I would recommend staying more Central to minimize walking in the heat. Seville is also very bike friendly and there are many scooters or self-service bikes for hire. Pick up and return your bike at any of a large number of stations around the city. Tips of visiting the Real Alcazar. Tour groups tend to visit the Royal Alcazar in the late morning or early afternoon so try to visit first thing or late afternoon to avoid peak hours. For good reason this is a really popular attraction so I would recommend booking your tickets online in advance although if you're visiting outside of peak hours then you might be okay to buy on the day. However if you know you're going to visit for sure and I think it's best to play it safe and book online before you go plus you will avoid the queue for the ticket office. Once inside the Alcazar you can stay for as long as you like. On average people spend around one and a half to two hours there although we were there for around three hours. Allocate longer for your visit if you opt for the additional tour of the upper Royal rooms. This add-on was unavailable for our visit so the decision was made for us although reviews are mixed as to whether or not it's actually worth it. When you book your ticket keep hold of it as it gets you into additional attractions in Seville such as the Ceramic Museum and the Antiquarium. When asked we were told that these tickets were still valid the next day so you don't have to try and cram these attractions into the same day as your visit to the Real Alcazar. Tips for visiting Las Setas. Las Setas is a large wooden mushroom-shaped structure that offers some good views of the city. if you do visit then be aware that there are several different ticket options. General access to the viewing platform during the day is 5 Euros which then goes up to 8 Euros if you visit from half an hour before sunset. Once the sun has gone down and the wooden walkways are transformed with an ambient sound and light show. The cost of a night ticket goes up to 10 euros. As we visited part way through the sunset window then we were charged for a night ticket but this did mean that we got to experience both the sunset and the light show which we were very happy with. You can either purchase your ticket online in advance or wait and buy them at the entrance to Las Setas either from a ticket machine or over the counter. know when purchasing your ticket that they also offer a more expensive ticket that includes an immersive film about the city so be aware that this is an optional extra. We didn't know this and when we bought our ticket over the counter they did charge us for this extra without asking as we didn't specify that we only wanted to visit the viewing platform so learn from our mistakes. Number eight is tips for visiting Seville Cathedral. When you buy your Cathedral ticket you'll see that you can purchase a combination ticket for the cathedral, the Bell Tower and the Church of the Divine Savior. If you don't book your tickets in advance then I would suggest buying it from the Church of the Divine Savior rather than the cathedral as the queue will be shorter. You could also book tickets for a cathedral rooftop tour which was a highlight of our visit to Seville. There's only a limited number of small group tours per day so if you would like to do a rooftop tour then I would recommend booking in advance. You may have to be flexible about when you fit the tour into your itinerary as it will only be available at a few set times throughout the day. Tours run in different languages so that's another reason to book ahead so that you can book a tour in a language you are fluent in. However reviews from people unable to book on a tour in their own language who then booked the Spanish one said it was still worth it to access the cathedral rooftops even without understanding the tour guide. Finally when visiting the Seville Cathedral be aware that there is a dress code so remember to bring something to cover your shoulders. Number nine is bring headphones. At a couple of attractions like Palacio De Las Duenas and Casa de Pilatos your entrance includes an audio guide that you can access using your phone. I would therefore recommend bringing a pair of headphones with you in case you want to listen to the guide. What if you visit on a Sunday? If your visit falls on a Sunday you may be worried that things could be closed. We found that with the exception of large supermarkets many shops were indeed shut but most attractions were open. Check opening hours of attractions as some may have limited hours on a Sunday or even Monday, but don't worry if your trip falls on a Sunday, there will still be plenty to do. Some restaurants were closed or had reduced hours on Sunday or Monday, but we found that many were operating as normal. Just check opening hours online in advance if there is somewhere in particular that you would like to dine. Despite many shops being closed on a Sunday we did find a few markets had popped up which was a great bonus of being in Seville on a Sunday. We stumbled across a couple of flea markets, one on the banks of the river of the Triana neighborhood where we were staying and another in a beautiful circular courtyard near the cathedral. If you're an art lover visiting Seville on a Sunday then head towards the Museum of Fine Arts where you'll find artists display their work outside the museum. These aren't the only markets held in Seville but these are the ones that we visited on a Sunday. For number 11 let's talk about food. Depending on where you're from lunchtime might be later than you're used to - it's typically from 2PM until 4pm - and dinner starts at around 9pm, although we found that many restaurants would start to open around one to two hours before this. If you do want to eat outside of these hours then you will still be able to find some places that will give you cold tapas while the kitchen is closed but your choice will be limited. Supermarkets will remain open so you can always grab something to tide you over. On your trip to Seville you're surely going to want to try the delicious local dishes like I've been showing you during this food segment and what a better way to do that than with tapas. By sharing small plates of food you'll get to try more different dishes and you can always order your favorites again. It also means that you can be more adventurous and try something you're unsure of because if you don't like it then hey - it's not your entire meal! How many plates per person will vary of course depending on your appetite and how filling each dish is, for example patatas bravas is particularly filling, but typically we would find three or four dishes would suit the two of us. While in some parts of Spain you may get tapa free with drink in Seville you will pay. We found dishes were usually around three or four Euros. Number 12 is the drinking water. It is perfectly safe to drink the tap water in Seville. Not only that but there are free water fountains in many squares and public spaces which is perfect given how hot Seville can get and how much walking you'll probably end up doing. Unfortunately public toilets are not something you'll find easily in Seville. The only ones we managed to find on our trip were paid public toilets just opposite Plaza de Espana plus some free toilets in Parque de Maria Louisa. While there may be others dotted around in our experience your best to take advantage of the facilities when you're at a tourist attraction or at a bar or restaurant. Number 14. Tipping at restaurants in Seville is not compulsory however it is certainly appreciated. We found that it was commonly suggested that you leave around 10% of a bill if the service was good. For bars or cafes we would typically round up our leave some change. Number 15, should you take cash or card? Card will be accepted in most places you go with perhaps the exception of some very small shops, paying on the bus or if you want to use those toilets we mentioned by Plaza de Espana. There may also be a minimum spend of a few Euros if you're paying on card in some small shops. I would therefore recommend planning to mainly use your card, ideally one that does not charge transaction fees when using it abroad, but then I would also take a small amount of cash with you. Even though you won't often need cash it's still always good to have some with you just in case you have any unexpected issues using your card. Number 16 is bug spray. Make sure you apply bug spray especially if you're visiting in the warmer months or you're spending time near the river. We didn't wear any for the first couple of days and soon really regretted it. Number 17. Plugs in Seville are the same as the majority of Europe consisting of two parallel pins so if you're traveling from the UK or outside of Europe then make sure you bring a travel adapter with you. Number 18. Walking around Seville you'll see the streets aligned with fragrant orange trees however although they may look tempting don't eat the oranges. They're actually very bitter so good for making marmalade but not so good for morning snack. Also keep your eyes peeled for oranges on the floor because, if you're clumsy like me, then you might end up stepping on one and getting sticky rotten orange all up your leg. Number 19 is will you be okay if you don't speak Spanish? In our experience yes you will be fine. We found at tourist sights and restaurants that there would usually be somebody there who could speak English. There were a couple of occasions where we needed the help of Google translate but overall getting by without speaking Spanish was absolutely fine. That being said we do always like to try and learn a few key phrases before we go and then even if our pronunciation is absolutely terrible we will have at least tried. It's also useful to memorise the names of food items you know you want to try so you can easily identify them on the menu. Number 20. Seville's location and excellent train connections make it a good base for easily exploring other popular destinations in Andalucia. The main train station in Seville is Santa Justa. You can book your ticket online in advance directly through the renfe website or through ticket retailers like trainline. Tickets could be released up to three months in advance but if you don't see trains for your route then keep checking back. When you book you may have the choice of either one of the high speed trains or one of the regional MD trains which are slower but also cheaper. If you want a half day trip out of the city but you don't want to go that far afield then you should visit the Roman ruins of Italica. Here you can walk through the excavated remains of the old Roman city and explore the amphitheater where Gladiators would battle in front of 25,000 spectators. In warmer months ago as early as you can because the site is very exposed so not many places to hide from the sun. I would also suggest starting with the ruined city and then moving on to the amphitheater as there is some shade in the tunnels. Make sure you bring plenty of water with you too. If you do want to visit Italica then it's an easy 30 minute bus ride from the Plaza De Armas bus station in Seville. There's no need to book a ticket in advance for either the ruins or the bus. If you have visited Seville and you have any other tips of your own then please do share them in the comments down below. Thanks for watching I hope this video was helpful

videos.transcript: Seville is a city of flamenco, unique Mudéjar architecture and rich Roman, Arab and Jewish heritage. After the discoveries of the Americas, Seville became the main port of trade between Europe and the New World and the city entered its golden age. And as a tour guide, I can't wait to show you the best things to do in Seville, Spain. To get a sense of Seville start on the top of the world's largest wooden structure, known as Mushrooms of Seville. This modern landmark in the heart of the old town provides amazing 360 views. The structure is made out of six interconnected giant mushroom like structures and has four levels. The underground level houses an archaeological museum with Roman and Moorish remains. Next to the museum entrance is an access to the viewing platform. First level is a street-level used as a farmers market. The roof of the first level is an open-air public square used for public events. Second and third levels are the two stages of the panoramic terraces has connected by the footbridges. In the evening terraces preform an impressive light show known as Aurora. Now you can dive in the largest old town in Europe. For centuries, Seville was a capital of muslim Spain, but was reconquered by Christians in the 1200s. Thanks to its important river port connected to the North Atlantic Ocean, Seville soon became the center for Spanish exploration. The conquest of America made Seville one of the wealthiest cities in Europe. The city became the economic center of the Spanish empire and to reflect its new status, a massive new city hall was built in the 15th century. Spacious square in front of the city hall is dominated by the statue of king Ferdinand III of Castile, who reconquered Seville from the Moors. From here, you can go in any direction to explore the old town that remains to be a commercial heart of the city. Right behind the city hall is the beginning of the most popular shopping street in Seville. Street has been a commercial heart of the city since the middle ages. At the time, it was filled with blacksmiths, cobblers and artisans of every kind. These days, it is dedicated only to pedestrians as the blacksmiths and cobblers were replaced by shops and cafes. You can turn left or right in any of the connecting streets as they're also incredibly charming. Everywhere you turn you will see numerous shops, cafes, tapas bars and restaurant spreading their tables around. Sooner or later, you'll end up on one of many charming old squares lined with beautiful houses, churches and palaces. In the northern part of the old town is one of the best nightlife areas that also happens to be connected to Seville's Roman past. Not particularly picturesque, but huge boulevard is dedicated to Roman hero Hercules. It starts with two original Roman columns, once part of the Roman temple devoted to him, as the legend goes that Hercules founded the city on 6 stone columns. Columns are topped with a sculpture of Hercules and Julius Caesar, who restored the city during Roman rule. The opposite end of the square is also adorned with two columns, topped with a lion and a coat of arms, one representing Spain and the other one Seville. Nowadays this area becomes alive in the evening when music performances draw people to the numerous bars and cafes lining this huge square. Seville also has the biggest and the most impressive bullfighting arena in Spain. Bullfighting is a spanish tradition with much contradiction, but this arena with 13.000 seats gets sold out each time there is a bullfight. On one bullfight, six bulls are performing and you get to see different teams of fighters. Bullfights can only be seen during bullfighting season that starts in April and ends in September. This impressive bullring is among the oldest and the finest in the world and if you don't want to watch a bullfight, it is worth just to visit the arena and its excellent museum that explores the history of bullfighting. Arena is very close to the river where you can find a beautiful riverside walkway dominated by one of Seville's most recognizable landmarks, a beautiful military watchtower from the 1200s. It was built by the Moors as part of their fortified city walls to control river access. There was a heavy chain that ran under water to the tower on the other side to prevent enemy ships from sailing on the river. The name Golden Tower refers to Seville's prosperous period during the times of the American colonies. When ships landed here, the tower was used to store Mayan and Incas gold. In addition to being a storage place, the middle part of the tower was used as a prison. Nowadays it houses a naval museum through which you can get to the roof terrace although the view is obstructed with Crenellation. Right on the other side of the river is a lively and colorful old neighborhood of Triana. This was once the commercial heart of Seville for pottery and production of Azulejos ceramic tiles with a typical blue painting. Nowadays there are surprisingly few tourists here as this is not the district with spectacular sights, but it is a perfect place for discovering churches, authentic shops, ceramics, and lots of tapas bars. This authentic neighborhood is interlaced with quiet, small streets and lively pedestrian areas that are simply a pleasure to wander through. In different places you might spot bullfighting posters as the most influential bullfighters of the last couple of centuries were born right in this neighborhood. Triana is also Seville's former Gypsy quarter where famous flamenco was born. Initially it started as a type of singing. Later the guitar was added and from the singing evolve the flamenco dance which developed over time. In Seville flamenco is performed almost on every corner. But to see a proper flamenco show look for one of many dedicated places called Tablao. Great shows are also available in the Teatro Flamenco, the House of Flamenco and in the Flamenco dance museum that also allows you to learn more about the history of this passionate art form. The most beautiful Andalusian palace in Seville was built as the biggest private residence by the Medinaceli family. Construction began in the late 1400s, but throughout the 16th century, the palace was remodeled in italian renaissance style with Mudéjar elements and decorations. Mudéjar style is unique and common in southern Spain as it developed as a meeting point between Christian and Muslim cultures. The term Mudéjar refers to the muslims who continue to practice their religion and customs also after Christian reconquest advanced into southern Spain. Remodeling of the palace in the middle of the 19th century completed its picturesque appearance. No wonder that this lovely building was used in several Hollywood productions. Palace is decorated with precious artwork and an impressive statue collection, including original Roman and Ancient Greek pieces, spread across courtyards and gardens. Part of the palace is still used as the residents of the 18th Duchess of Medinaceli and her family, while the rest of the house is open to the public year-round. The most famous, beautiful and popular park in Seville is home to many monuments, ponds, and fountains. Park also serves as a botanical garden and on its edge is the most famous square in Seville, Plaza de España. The story of the square dates to 1929 when Seville hosted a world's fair called the Ibero-American exhibition. In preparation for the exhibition, the entire southern end of the city was redeveloped, including the park. On the edge of the park great Seville architect Aníbal González built this spectacular square that immediately became one of the landmarks of Seville. The shape of the square with its main building and two towers represents the embrace of Spain and its ancient colonies as it is oriented facing the river as a path to America. The square is surrounded by a long canal crossed by four bridges representing the four ancient kingdoms of Spain. There is also a series of 48 benches with Andalusian tiles, each representing one Spanish province. No wonder that this amazing square was featured in several movies like Lawrence of Arabia and Star Wars - Attack of the clones. But the city's tourist heart is the Santa Cruz neighborhood. Heart of historic Seville was once the biggest Jewish quarter in Spain. Here are some of the most important sights like the Cathedral and Royal Palace. But besides sightseeing, this picturesque area is also one of the best places for aimless wandering. Narrow streets provide protection from the summer sun. They are lined with historic buildings and houses, offering a wide variety of tapas bars, restaurants and shops selling souvenirs, hand crafts, and ceramics. A labyrinth of narrow streets will take you to charming squares lined with orange trees. But one of the most romantic parts is Calle Agua street that runs alongside the walls of the Royal Palace and leads to the beautiful Alfaro and Santa Cruz squares. But the most impressive part of Santa Cruz is Plaza del Triunfo, a small square surrounded by the most important and popular Seville attractions. First one is the Royal palace, with the entrance right over there, the second is the Cathedral right here and the third one is stuck between them. It is called the General Archive of the Indies and for most its name doesn't reveal much. This beautiful spanish renaissance building from the 1500s and its contents are a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Its prime location suggests its high importance as originally it was built as the Merchants' Exchange. All voyages of explorations and trade to the New World had to be approved here. That is why Seville could keep the control over the entire wealth transported from the New World. But since the 1700s this government building has served as an archive of the colonial history of the New World, hence its name. The archive, with more than 6,5 miles of bookshelves, contains 80 million pages of documents, photographs, drawings, and diaries from the likes of Columbus, Pizarro and Cortés. Right next door is Seville Cathedral, the largest gothic cathedral in the world and third largest church in Europe. But before the city was retaken by the Christians this used to be a great mosque of Seville. Christians converted the mosque into a cathedral, but a century later the building was badly damaged by an earthquake. Plans for a new cathedral were made and they had to reflect Seville's new position as a major trading center between Europe and the New World. After the discovery of Americas, Seville became the economic center of the Spanish empire and the main European port of connection with the New World. That is why in 1506 the largest church in the world was built here. This UNESCO World Heritage Site has several beautiful doors that lead to an impressive interior with an extremely high gothic vault ceiling. Builders preserve some elements of the original mosque, most notably a square floor plan instead of the customary Latin cross and a bell tower that was originally built as a minaret. Church holds many great artworks created by world-class masters, but one of the artistic highlights is a golden gothic altar made of 45 panels representing the life of Christ. Vast interior has about 80 chapels, and some of them contains the remains of Spanish kings. But on the most prominent spot you will see an impressive tomb of Christopher Columbus, as Seville reached its golden days thanks to his discoveries. Columbus remains in the casket are borne by kings of Castile, Leon, Aragon and Navarre. The bell tower of the Cathedral is probably the best meeting point of Christian and Muslim cultures and one of the most important symbols of Seville. The tower was originally built in the 1100s as the minaret for the Great Mosque of Seville. Soon after its completion the Christians reconquered Seville and added a belfry on the top of the minaret. In the 1500s the tower got a new renaissance style extension and reached its current height of 342 feet. You can climb to the top by walking a ramp with 35 turns although the view is obstructed and the bells are very loud. No building in the city center is allowed to be taller than this bell tower. Its name, La Giralda, comes from a rotating vane sculpture on its top known as the Giraldillo. Interestingly the city of Marrakech has a twin tower just like the Giralda and you can compare it if you watch my Marrakech video in the top right corner. Finally here is the most famous Seville attraction, the oldest European royal palace still in use today. You could admire it in the TV series Game of Thrones as this UNESCO World Heritage Site was built over eleven centuries. It was first built in the 900s as a residential fortress by the Moors as Seville was a capital of muslim Spain. The fortress was badly damaged during Christian reconquest in the 1200s. Christian kings seemed impressed by the Arab architecture as they transformed the remains of the Moorish fortress into a collection of royal palaces adorned with Mudéjar ornamentation and decoration. Part of Mudéjar style is also famous tile decoration with geometric design inspired by arabesque ornamentation. The most spectacularly decorated part of the complex is Palacio de Don Pedro. Here you'll find a famous Courtyard of the Maidens, a superb example of an islamic inner-courtyard. It is centered with a reflection pool and fountain, encircled with arcades. You also shouldn't miss Ambassador's Hall, the most elaborately decorated room in the palace, used as the throne room. Its highlight is exquisitely decorate celling, an excellent example of Mudéjar style design arranged in geometric artwork. As the palace has many more impressive parts with minimal information displayed around the site, having some sort of guide is essential. Audio guide is available at the entrance, but I would recommend you to book a guided tour in the description below. There you'll also find my other favorite tours, tickets, and experiences. My name is Rok, thanks for the thumbs up and for watching, and see you next time!

videos.title: Livability for Expats: The Pros & Cons of Seville Spain
videos.description:
This city has been on our list of must-visit places since long before we knew each other and we were excited to finally spend some time here.

Seville Spain is a romantic European city with tons of history. It’s located in the Andalusian region of southwest Spain and people have been living in the area for over 2800 years. 

It was ruled by the Romans for 700 years and then by the Moors for 800 years. Even the Vikings ruled it for 40 days in the year 844! Spain reclaimed it in the late 1400s under Queen Isabela. 

Today, Seville is a popular place to visit and live due to its ancient history, rich culture, and modern amenities. We felt right at home meandering through the streets, eating at restaurants, shopping for food, and enjoying tapas at street-side cafes.

In this video, we’ll share our first impressions from our exploratory trip and take a look at the pros & cons of Seville as a place to live abroad.

videos.title: Top 5 Mistakes to Avoid When Visiting Seville, Spain (Essential Travel Tips!)
videos.description:
Planning a trip to Seville, Spain? Don’t make the same mistakes we did! Join our family as we explore the golden treasures of Andalucia, with Seville as one of the crown jewels. In this video, we visit the iconic Plaza de España, marvel at the grandeur of the Seville Cathedral, climb the historic La Giralda Tower, and attempt to explore the Royal Alcázar of Seville—but not everything went as planned.

From timing mishaps to underestimating Seville's charm, we share 5 key mistakes to avoid when visiting this breathtaking city. Whether you’re planning a family trip, a solo adventure, or a romantic getaway, this guide will help you make the most of your Seville experience.

✨ Why Seville is a Must-See Destination
Seville is the beating heart of southern Spain, offering a vibrant mix of culture, history, and architectural wonders. From the majestic Plaza de España, featured in iconic films like Star Wars, to the intricate details of the Alcázar, Seville never fails to amaze. And let’s not forget the Seville Cathedral, one of the largest Gothic cathedrals in the world, home to Christopher Columbus’ tomb.

But as beautiful as it is, Seville can also present a few challenges for first-time visitors. That’s where this video comes in—helping you navigate potential pitfalls and maximize your trip.

🌟 What We Cover in This Video

Plaza de España: A breathtaking blend of Spanish architecture, stunning tilework, and cultural charm.
Royal Alcázar of Seville: A UNESCO World Heritage site with captivating Moorish designs—but we made a big mistake here you’ll want to avoid!
Seville Cathedral & La Giralda: The architectural marvels that define Seville’s skyline.
Flamenco Culture: Dive into Seville’s vibrant traditions by attending a local Flamenco show.
Travel Tips: Learn from our experiences to avoid common tourist traps and make your visit unforgettable.
🌞 Mistakes to Avoid in Seville

Booking Attractions on the Wrong Day: Timing is everything—don’t get caught out like we did!
Trying to See It All in One Day: Seville’s rich offerings deserve more time than you might think.
Making Seville Your First Stop: Find out why we recommend starting elsewhere in Andalucia.
Missing Local Events: Seville’s festivals and events are integral to its charm—don’t miss out!
Visiting in the Summer Heat: Learn why spring or fall is a better choice for exploring the city.
💡 Pro Tip: Save time by booking tickets online for major attractions like the Alcázar and the Cathedral to avoid long queues.

🎥 Video Chapters for Easy Navigation

👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 About Us
We’re a full-time travel family exploring the world, one adventure at a time. Seville was a hit for all of us, and we’re confident it will be for you too! From bustling cities to hidden gems, we’re on a mission to share our discoveries and tips to inspire your next journey.
